title:
House - Health and Human Services (Room 307)
House Health and Human Services
Adjourned
2026-02-18 09:38-10:16

speaker_map.name: Melissa Salazar
speaker_map.role: Director of Boards and Commissions, RLD

summary: The committee heard SB 246, which proposes licensing and inspection requirements for massage therapy establishments to combat human trafficking and ensure hygiene. Significant debate occurred regarding the privacy of clients during unannounced inspections, with several members expressing concern over inspectors entering rooms while sessions are in progress. The bill received a 'do pass' recommendation on a 5-4 vote.

turns.speaker: Committee Clerk
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair. Representative Anaya, present. Representative Cate, present. Representative Travis. Representative Ferrary. Representative Jones, here.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: Welcome to our last meeting of House Health and Human Services for this interim. We will hear SB 246 as amended. Go ahead, Senator.

turns.speaker: Senator Pat Woods
turns.text: Thank you very much, Madam Chair, for holding this last meeting and allowing me to bring a bill. I appreciate the accommodation you did for me. This bill goes to the licensing of massage therapy. Right now, we don't have much control over that group. We're trying to pull it together. When I get home, there's going to be some people on me because they think, 'Why should we regulate anything?' But it's getting to the point in time, Madam Chair, that there's been some prostitution rings. To keep the legitimacy of this group together so that the public knows that it's a place you can go and get some pain relief or physical therapy, that's kind of the bill in a nutshell. I've got Melissa Salazar here, the Director of Boards and Commissions at RLD, to act as an expert witness.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: Madam Chair, if you could go ahead and introduce yourself and tell us what your title is.

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: Good morning, Madam Chair, members of the committee. I am Melissa Salazar. I'm the Director of Boards and Commissions at Regulation and Licensing.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: Thank you. With that, I will go to the audience. If you are in opposition on Zoom, raise your hand. Anyone here in opposition? Okay, seeing none—I think the public is getting as tired as we are. There's only two this morning in support. If you are on Zoom in support, raise your hand. In the room in support, go ahead and come forward. We'll stick with our one minute.

turns.speaker: Jason Thompson
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair, members of the committee. My name is Jason Thompson. I'm here representing the New Mexico chapter of the American Massage Therapy Association. We're officially in a neutral position on this, but we want just to thank the sponsors and RLD for always communicating with us and hearing out our issues. Our primary concerns and issues that we wanted to press on the bill—if this does pass and get signed by the Governor—is just stressing the importance of the rulemaking process here to delegate the issues around the inspections and licensure to the Massage Therapy Board, who will be the folks responsible for making sure this is done in a thorough way with the folks that are actually therapists that do this for a living. So we appreciate them taking our considerations into consideration and for working with us on that. Thank you.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: Thank you, and I don't see any hands raised. Senator, do you have any follow-up, or are you ready for questions? Representative Jones, go ahead.

turns.speaker: Representative Jones
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair. Thank you, Senator. Did we hear this bill last year, or something like this last year? I'm just curious about the history and where we've been in the past with this issue.

turns.speaker: Senator Pat Woods
turns.text: Representative, thank you for that question. I'm going to let my expert witness answer that because I do not have the background on that.

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative Jones, there was a bill introduced last session similar to this one, but it was not brought before this committee.

turns.speaker: Representative Jones
turns.text: Okay, thank you. I know that we had something like this; I just remember some interesting conversation around this issue. So thank you very much.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: Thank you. And Representative, we have a lot of interesting conversations in this committee. Representative Cate.

turns.speaker: Representative Kathleen Cate
turns.text: Thank you, Chair, and thank you, Senator, for bringing this forward. I do have a few questions. Currently, massage therapy is licensed under the RLD. Is this pertaining to the licensed massage therapy facilities? Is that correct?

turns.speaker: Senator Pat Woods
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative Cate, I think this is more about the establishment than the individual licensing, but it also has something about renewal of license. The way I read the bill—and I probably stepped off into the deep end there—I'll let my expert answer that question.

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative Cate, the Regulation and Licensing Department currently only licenses the massage therapists. That's what we have authority for currently.

turns.speaker: Representative Kathleen Cate
turns.text: Thank you, Chair, and thank you for that answer; that does enlighten me. So you only have a license for the therapist, not the facility. Are the facilities licensed under the Department of Health possibly, or is there currently no license for the facility itself?

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative Cate, currently they do not have any oversight. There is no licensure for establishments for massage therapists.

turns.speaker: Representative Kathleen Cate
turns.text: Thank you so much, Chair. So the sticking point in this bill is that you're asking for inspectors under the RLD or under the Massage Therapy Board? Where are the inspectors coming from? And do we have the inspectors available?

turns.speaker: Senator Pat Woods
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative, we're going to have to train some more inspectors, but there's not that many more that we need. From previous testimony, there are not very many FTEs needed because this is going to spread out over the state, and they inspect them when they come to town.

turns.speaker: Representative Kathleen Cate
turns.text: Thank you very much, Senator. Can your expert answer if the inspectors are under RLD or under the Massage Therapy Board?

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative, the inspector would fall under the Regulation and Licensing Department.

turns.speaker: Representative Kathleen Cate
turns.text: Thank you very much. I understand the need for us to be able to inspect the facilities themselves, most certainly. But in this bill, it gives the right for the inspector to not just come in unannounced, which I understand the need for, but to actually enter the rooms where the client is at, unannounced. In public comment, it was brought to our committee's attention to 'let the board propagate rules to address this,' but what would those rules look like? This is my issue: if I'm a patient in a teaching hospital, the doctor enters and asks for my consent regarding other people being in the room. What you want to achieve is an accountability mechanism for sex trafficking, but you can't ask permission in the beginning. What would the rules look like to maintain my privacy while providing a tool for law enforcement?

turns.speaker: Senator Pat Woods
turns.text: Thank you, Representative. Madam Chair, the expert witness will lay that out for you.

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative, upon review of what other states with regulatory oversight have incorporated, for example, for a home-based establishment, they provide that if an inspector comes upon an establishment and that provider is currently in session, their rules provide that the inspector has to reschedule and come back. It's going to be dependent on the rules that the board promulgates surrounding the criteria for inspection. Based on what I have seen in other states, they have laid out very thoughtfully what's allowed to occur during their inspections. I believe that working with the Massage Therapy Board, we would be able to have discussions regarding how we can best accommodate these requests or concerns providers have with respect to inspections happening while they're providing services.

turns.speaker: Representative Kathleen Cate
turns.text: Thank you, Chair. So you're telling me other states have rules where if a client is there, they have to come back. But that's not what this law says. This law says they're allowed to come there when the client is there. What is the purpose of this law? It seems to me it is to ensure facilities are clean and sanitary and to reduce opportunities of sex trafficking. Are those the two missions, or am I missing something?

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative, yes, you're absolutely correct. We are looking out for the safety of the public as well as looking to identify if there is any human trafficking occurring. But our primary concern at RLD is to protect the public in terms of public hygiene, making sure they're using clean linens after every service, those types of things.

turns.speaker: Representative Kathleen Cate
turns.text: Thank you, Chair. Senator, this is your last stop before the floor. Was there any proposed amendment to roll out the ability to inspect in the middle of a session? If that portion was not part of this bill, I think we would already be downstairs voting. Has anybody proposed an amendment to address this? An inspector is appropriate for cleanliness, but they're not a tool for law enforcement unless you create a rule making them obligated to notify law enforcement of suspicion. Being able to come in in the middle of a session—I would not like that.

turns.speaker: Senator Pat Woods
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative, there's been a lot of talk about that issue right there. In this particular bill, we talk about the rules being made. I agree; you shouldn't be able to enter a dressing room while personal things are going on. You'd be surprised how many people noted that, but they offered no amendments. In every committee, those were the sticking points—that you've got the right to go in a personal home and inspect anytime you want to. That's a little bit pushing the ball a little bit too far, but that's the way it's written.

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative, the legislation is purposely overbroad as to the requirements for the reason that the board, like many other boards regulated by RLD, is tasked with promulgating rules. For example, the Barber and Cosmetology Board rules specifically say inspections can only occur during regular business posted hours. Our inspectors are very mindful of what is happening. If they walk into a cosmetology location and a facial is being performed, they're aware those chemicals can only be on the patient for a certain time. They will speak to the provider and say they will reschedule. We conduct similar inspections for Body Art and other boards during regular business hours, all under rules promulgated by those boards.

turns.speaker: Representative Kathleen Cate
turns.text: Thank you, Chair, but I am not naked when I'm getting my hair cut. I think that's a big difference. Senator, I wish we had a friendly amendment for the floor on page 6, line 11, where it says 'customers are present' to add 'but not in the service room that the customer is occupied in.' I understand that limits the inspection, and I understand that might go against your purpose as a law enforcement tool, but I just don't see an inspector as an appropriate tool for law enforcement. I see the importance of the bill; I just wish we had time to work on it.

turns.speaker: Representative Anaya
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair, and thank you, Senator. It's a topic I care a lot about, but I would like to express the same concerns as Representative Cate. For all other licenses with RLD, you're not necessarily naked. I think this requires minimums in statute. I take issue with RLD becoming a type of eye for law enforcement. Why isn't law enforcement tackling these massage parlor sex trafficking rings?

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: Representative, I have talked with law enforcement about this. What they tell me is that particularly in trafficking, no one will speak. They have no way to prove anything because passports are being held or families are being threatened. It's really sticky.

turns.speaker: Representative Anaya
turns.text: Madam Chair and Senator, my assumption is that if law enforcement believes something's happening, they can enter a facility in the same way this bill writes for RLD. Is that correct?

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: Representative, again, what I have been told is they kind of find it as a waste of time because they go in and everybody will not speak to them.

turns.speaker: Senator Pat Woods
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative, real-world circumstances are that if you don't have proof of prostitution but you can go in there on a violation of rules, maybe you could shut something down that way. To say this is going to eliminate prostitution? No. But it's going to put more eyes on the establishment, and I think that's the crux of it.

turns.speaker: Representative Anaya
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair. I want to draw a distinction between prostitution and sex trafficking, as they are two different things. I don't believe this will make things any different if RLD comes in and no one talks. I would appreciate if this bill had more work, and I would consider co-sponsoring it if it had minimum safety and privacy standards for customers. I don't think I can support it in its current form.

turns.speaker: Representative Herndon
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair. Director Salazar, regarding the rules, I don't think you would be able to create a rule that is the opposite of statutory language. If the bill passes with this language, a rule requiring inspectors to leave when clients are present might not be upheld in court. You might want to consider amending this and coming back next year. Also, does RLD license every single facility for all 29 boards and commissions?

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative, no. We only license establishments for Barber and Cosmetology, Body Art, and Funeral establishments.

turns.speaker: Representative Herndon
turns.text: And are the inspectors law enforcement officers?

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: The inspectors are hired by RLD and work specifically for the Boards and Commissions Division.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: I'm going to jump in here. Many other licensees, like physical therapists or occupational therapists, are licensed by a different authority, like the Healthcare Authority. RLD does not do the licensing for those.

turns.speaker: Representative Herndon
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair. My question was about the background of these inspectors. Are they law enforcement officers?

turns.speaker: Senator Pat Woods
turns.text: Representative, there's different training for each inspector. I'm not sure we've ever given policing authority to Regulation and Licensing. If you do that, you have to go through the Academy, which is a lot more training. I suppose we could get you those handbooks if you'd like to see them.

turns.speaker: Representative Herndon
turns.text: Thank you. One last question from constituents: Do you have 24/7 entry into every establishment licensed under Boards and Commissions?

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative, no. For Barber and Cosmetology or Body Art, the statute gives the ability to inspect, but the rules create the guideline which says we can enter during posted hours.

turns.speaker: Representative Herndon
turns.text: Thank you. And will there be a cost associated with the licensing of the establishment?

turns.speaker: Senator Pat Woods
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative, I believe there possibly could be, but again, that is a decision of the board that would be promulgated by rule.

turns.speaker: Representative Nicole Chavez
turns.text: Thank you, Madam Chair. I understand the intention. I just had one question regarding acupuncturists. I was told they were excluded or should be exempt. Can you clarify that?

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: Representative, this is massage therapy. I'll let the expert clarify.

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative, no, they are not included in this bill.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: If and when this bill passes, I know most boards are self-funded. Would the Massage Therapy Board be able to absorb the costs of inspectors through these fees?

turns.speaker: Melissa Salazar
turns.text: Madam Chair, yes, I believe so through the fee that would be charged for the establishment license. Again, it is up to the board to decide what those fees are.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: I will entertain a motion. I have a 'do pass' from Representative Jones.

turns.speaker: Representative Alan Martinez
turns.text: Second. Madam Chair, Senator, it's good to see you. My question is on page 8, line 13. The date of February 4th, 2019 seems like it was pulled out of the air. Why that date?

turns.speaker: Senator Pat Woods
turns.text: Madam Chair, Representative Martinez, they thought it was good language the way it was written in that 2019 Act and that the effective date should remain the same. The analysis I saw indicated it was related to existing law.

turns.speaker: Committee Clerk
turns.text: Representative Anaya: No. Representative Cate: Pass. Representative Chavez (E): Yes. Representative Chavez (N): No. Representative Ferrary: Pass. Representative Jones: Yes. Representative Martinez: Yes. Representative Cate: Yes. Representative Ferrary: No. Madam Chair: Yes.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: It's a five to four. You have a five-to-four 'do pass.' Senator, I know you will take heed to what has been said in here.

turns.speaker: Representative Kathleen Cate
turns.text: Thank you, Chair. Senator, I understand the intentions and your integrity. This needs to be amended. I hope to work with you; otherwise, I'm getting a heads-up that there will be an amendment on the floor this afternoon.

turns.speaker: Senator Pat Woods
turns.text: Madam Chair, you didn't have to do this. I thank you so much for coming in when you didn't have to. The chances of it going across the floor are slim now, but you gave me more input on this bill, and we're going to try to make this thing better.

turns.speaker: Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson
turns.text: Senator, we are a hardworking committee. We got ten bills out in the first week. We wouldn't have done it for any other senator. So, thank you all very much. Obviously, this will be our last meeting. It's been a pleasure having all of you. We stand adjourned.

full_text:
[Committee Clerk]: Thank you, Madam Chair. Representative Anaya, present. Representative Cate, present. Representative Travis. Representative Ferrary. Representative Jones, here.

[Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson]: Welcome to our last meeting of House Health and Human Services for this interim. We will hear SB 246 as amended. Go ahead, Senator.

[Senator Pat Woods]: Thank you very much, Madam Chair, for holding this last meeting and allowing me to bring a bill. I appreciate the accommodation you did for me. This bill goes to the licensing of massage therapy. Right now, we don't have much control over that group. We're trying to pull it together. When I get home, there's going to be some people on me because they think, 'Why should we regulate anything?' But it's getting to the point in time, Madam Chair, that there's been some prostitution rings. To keep the legitimacy of this group together so that the public knows that it's a place you can go and get some pain relief or physical therapy, that's kind of the bill in a nutshell. I've got Melissa Salazar here, the Director of Boards and Commissions at RLD, to act as an expert witness.

[Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson]: Madam Chair, if you could go ahead and introduce yourself and tell us what your title is.

[Melissa Salazar]: Good morning, Madam Chair, members of the committee. I am Melissa Salazar. I'm the Director of Boards and Commissions at Regulation and Licensing.

Okay, seeing none—I think the public is getting as tired as we are. There's only two this morning in support. If you are on Zoom in support, raise your hand. In the room in support, go ahead and come forward. We'll stick with our one minute.

[Jason Thompson]: Thank you, Madam Chair, members of the committee. My name is Jason Thompson. I'm here representing the New Mexico chapter of the American Massage Therapy Association. We're officially in a neutral position on this, but we want just to thank the sponsors and RLD for always communicating with us and hearing out our issues. Our primary concerns and issues that we wanted to press on the bill—if this does pass and get signed by the Governor—is just stressing the importance of the rulemaking process here to delegate the issues around the inspections and licensure to the Massage Therapy Board, who will be the folks responsible for making sure this is done in a thorough way with the folks that are actually therapists that do this for a living. So we appreciate them taking our considerations into consideration and for working with us on that. Thank you.

[Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson]: Thank you, and I don't see any hands raised. Senator, do you have any follow-up, or are you ready for questions? Representative Jones, go ahead.

[Representative Jones]: Thank you, Madam Chair. Thank you, Senator. Did we hear this bill last year, or something like this last year? I'm just curious about the history and where we've been in the past with this issue.

[Senator Pat Woods]: Representative, thank you for that question. I'm going to let my expert witness answer that because I do not have the background on that.

[Melissa Salazar]: Madam Chair, Representative Jones, there was a bill introduced last session similar to this one, but it was not brought before this committee.

[Representative Jones]: Okay, thank you. I know that we had something like this; I just remember some interesting conversation around this issue. So thank you very much.

[Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson]: Thank you. And Representative, we have a lot of interesting conversations in this committee. Representative Cate.

[Representative Kathleen Cate]: Thank you, Chair, and thank you, Senator, for bringing this forward. I do have a few questions. Currently, massage therapy is licensed under the RLD. Is this pertaining to the licensed massage therapy facilities? Is that correct?

[Senator Pat Woods]: Madam Chair, Representative Cate, I think this is more about the establishment than the individual licensing, but it also has something about renewal of license. The way I read the bill—and I probably stepped off into the deep end there—I'll let my expert answer that question.

[Melissa Salazar]: Madam Chair, Representative Cate, the Regulation and Licensing Department currently only licenses the massage therapists. That's what we have authority for currently.

[Representative Kathleen Cate]: Thank you, Chair, and thank you for that answer; that does enlighten me. So you only have a license for the therapist, not the facility. Are the facilities licensed under the Department of Health possibly, or is there currently no license for the facility itself?

[Melissa Salazar]: Madam Chair, Representative Cate, currently they do not have any oversight. There is no licensure for establishments for massage therapists.

[Representative Kathleen Cate]: Thank you so much, Chair. So the sticking point in this bill is that you're asking for inspectors under the RLD or under the Massage Therapy Board? Where are the inspectors coming from? And do we have the inspectors available?

[Senator Pat Woods]: Madam Chair, Representative, we're going to have to train some more inspectors, but there's not that many more that we need. From previous testimony, there are not very many FTEs needed because this is going to spread out over the state, and they inspect them when they come to town.

[Representative Kathleen Cate]: Thank you very much, Senator. Can your expert answer if the inspectors are under RLD or under the Massage Therapy Board?

[Representative Kathleen Cate]: Thank you very much. I understand the need for us to be able to inspect the facilities themselves, most certainly. Being able to come in in the middle of a session—I would not like that.

[Senator Pat Woods]: Madam Chair, Representative, there's been a lot of talk about that issue right there. In this particular bill, we talk about the rules being made. I agree; you shouldn't be able to enter a dressing room while personal things are going on. You'd be surprised how many people noted that, but they offered no amendments. In every committee, those were the sticking points—that you've got the right to go in a personal home and inspect anytime you want to. [Melissa Salazar]: Madam Chair, Representative, the legislation is purposely overbroad as to the requirements for the reason that the board, like many other boards regulated by RLD, is tasked with promulgating rules. [Representative Kathleen Cate]: Thank you, Chair, but I am not naked when I'm getting my hair cut. I think that's a big difference. Senator, I wish we had a friendly amendment for the floor on page 6, line 11, where it says 'customers are present' to add 'but not in the service room that the customer is occupied in.' I understand that limits the inspection, and I understand that might go against your purpose as a law enforcement tool, but I just don't see an inspector as an appropriate tool for law enforcement. I see the importance of the bill; I just wish we had time to work on it.

[Representative Anaya]: Thank you, Madam Chair, and thank you, Senator. It's a topic I care a lot about, but I would like to express the same concerns as Representative Cate. For all other licenses with RLD, you're not necessarily naked. I think this requires minimums in statute. I take issue with RLD becoming a type of eye for law enforcement. [Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson]: Representative, I have talked with law enforcement about this. What they tell me is that particularly in trafficking, no one will speak. They have no way to prove anything because passports are being held or families are being threatened. It's really sticky.

[Representative Anaya]: Madam Chair and Senator, my assumption is that if law enforcement believes something's happening, they can enter a facility in the same way this bill writes for RLD. Is that correct?

[Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson]: Representative, again, what I have been told is they kind of find it as a waste of time because they go in and everybody will not speak to them.

[Senator Pat Woods]: Madam Chair, Representative, real-world circumstances are that if you don't have proof of prostitution but you can go in there on a violation of rules, maybe you could shut something down that way. [Representative Anaya]: Thank you, Madam Chair. I want to draw a distinction between prostitution and sex trafficking, as they are two different things. I don't believe this will make things any different if RLD comes in and no one talks. I would appreciate if this bill had more work, and I would consider co-sponsoring it if it had minimum safety and privacy standards for customers. I don't think I can support it in its current form.

[Representative Herndon]: Thank you, Madam Chair. Director Salazar, regarding the rules, I don't think you would be able to create a rule that is the opposite of statutory language. If the bill passes with this language, a rule requiring inspectors to leave when clients are present might not be upheld in court. You might want to consider amending this and coming back next year. Also, does RLD license every single facility for all 29 boards and commissions?

[Melissa Salazar]: Madam Chair, Representative, no. We only license establishments for Barber and Cosmetology, Body Art, and Funeral establishments.

[Representative Herndon]: And are the inspectors law enforcement officers?

[Melissa Salazar]: The inspectors are hired by RLD and work specifically for the Boards and Commissions Division.

[Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson]: I'm going to jump in here. Many other licensees, like physical therapists or occupational therapists, are licensed by a different authority, like the Healthcare Authority. RLD does not do the licensing for those.

[Representative Herndon]: Thank you, Madam Chair. My question was about the background of these inspectors. Are they law enforcement officers?

[Senator Pat Woods]: Representative, there's different training for each inspector. I'm not sure we've ever given policing authority to Regulation and Licensing. If you do that, you have to go through the Academy, which is a lot more training. I suppose we could get you those handbooks if you'd like to see them.

[Representative Herndon]: Thank you. One last question from constituents: Do you have 24/7 entry into every establishment licensed under Boards and Commissions?

[Melissa Salazar]: Madam Chair, Representative, no. For Barber and Cosmetology or Body Art, the statute gives the ability to inspect, but the rules create the guideline which says we can enter during posted hours.

[Representative Herndon]: Thank you. And will there be a cost associated with the licensing of the establishment?

[Senator Pat Woods]: Madam Chair, Representative, I believe there possibly could be, but again, that is a decision of the board that would be promulgated by rule.

[Representative Nicole Chavez]: Thank you, Madam Chair. I understand the intention. I just had one question regarding acupuncturists. I was told they were excluded or should be exempt. Can you clarify that?

[Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson]: Representative, this is massage therapy. I'll let the expert clarify.

[Melissa Salazar]: Madam Chair, Representative, no, they are not included in this bill.

[Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson]: If and when this bill passes, I know most boards are self-funded. Would the Massage Therapy Board be able to absorb the costs of inspectors through these fees?

[Melissa Salazar]: Madam Chair, yes, I believe so through the fee that would be charged for the establishment license. Again, it is up to the board to decide what those fees are.

[Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson]: I will entertain a motion. I have a 'do pass' from Representative Jones.

[Representative Alan Martinez]: Second. Madam Chair, Senator, it's good to see you. My question is on page 8, line 13. The date of February 4th, 2019 seems like it was pulled out of the air. Why that date?

[Senator Pat Woods]: Madam Chair, Representative Martinez, they thought it was good language the way it was written in that 2019 Act and that the effective date should remain the same. The analysis I saw indicated it was related to existing law.

[Committee Clerk]: Representative Anaya: No. Representative Cate: Pass. Representative Chavez (E): Yes. Representative Chavez (N): No. Representative Ferrary: Pass. Representative Jones: Yes. Representative Martinez: Yes. Representative Cate: Yes. Representative Ferrary: No. Madam Chair: Yes.

[Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson]: It's a five to four. You have a five-to-four 'do pass.' Senator, I know you will take heed to what has been said in here.

[Representative Kathleen Cate]: Thank you, Chair. Senator, I understand the intentions and your integrity. This needs to be amended. I hope to work with you; otherwise, I'm getting a heads-up that there will be an amendment on the floor this afternoon.

[Senator Pat Woods]: Madam Chair, you didn't have to do this. I thank you so much for coming in when you didn't have to. The chances of it going across the floor are slim now, but you gave me more input on this bill, and we're going to try to make this thing better.

[Chair Rep. Elizabeth Thomson]: Senator, we are a hardworking committee. We got ten bills out in the first week. We wouldn't have done it for any other senator. So, thank you all very much. Obviously, this will be our last meeting. It's been a pleasure having all of you. We stand adjourned.
